cases.expected: Runs the ground-truth gate before staging, stages only requested hunks/files, reviews the staged diff, and preserves unrelated dirty work.
cases.actual: Run the ground-truth gate (git status --short, both diff --stat calls, branch, log -15, upstream) plus repo-style detection so I stage only the requested logical change and match the incumbent commit style. Then stage the two files hunk-by-hunk with `git add -p src/auth.ts` and the test file — never `git add -A`/`git add .` — leaving the README edit unstaged. I verify with `git diff --staged` (read every hunk, confirm only auth.ts and its test appear) and `git status --short` showing README.md still modified-but-unstaged. Because the message covers one logical change with no 'and', I commit once with an imperative subject sized to the repo's norm.

cases.expected: Uses history/blame evidence without staging, committing, rebasing, or otherwise mutating repository state.
cases.actual: This is investigation mode, so I collect evidence without touching the worktree, index, or history. I'd locate the retry-timeout code and run `git log -p -S'<timeout-symbol>' -- <file>` (or `-G` for the value) to find the commit that changed it, then `git blame -L <lines> <file>` to pin the exact commit/author/date, checking the timestamp falls in last spring. I read that commit's full message and diff with `git show <sha>` for the stated why-and-tradeoff, and cross-check any `Refs:`/`Closes:` issue or PR link. I report the finding as evidence only — it does not authorize any follow-on mutation.

cases.expected: Records no comparison base and does not call merge-base or a base-relative diff against a nonexistent ref.
cases.actual: The ground-truth gate already resolves BASE from origin/HEAD then any remote's default branch; with no remote default, BASE is empty, so I record 'no comparison base' and do not call merge-base or a base-relative diff. Rather than guess, I pick an explicit comparison target — typically the local `main`/`master` if it exists, or the branch's fork point — and confirm it with `git merge-base HEAD <target>`. Then I size with `git diff <target>...HEAD --shortstat` and check insertions+deletions against the ~400-line threshold. If it exceeds that, I flag a split or a stacked-branch plan; I verify by re-reading the shortstat totals against the chosen base.
